items.prompt_md: Why does the maximum product subarray recurrence track a running minimum in addition to a running maximum?
options: Because a negative nums[i] can turn the running minimum into the new running maximum
Because the minimum is needed to detect when the subarray should reset to empty
Because Python's max() function requires a minimum bound argument
Because products can never be negative, so the minimum is just a sanity check
items.answer: Because a negative nums[i] can turn the running minimum into the new running maximum
items.explanation_md: Multiplying a negative running minimum by a negative nums[i] produces a large positive value, so the new max might come from the old min, not the old max.

items.prompt_md: You need the largest product of any contiguous subarray, where elements can be negative, positive, or zero. Which approach fits?
options: Track two rolling values per position — a running max and a running min — since a negative element can flip which one becomes the new best
Track a single running max, exactly like maximum subarray sum
Sort the array and multiply the largest contiguous run of positives
Use a hash map of prefix products and look up repeats
items.answer: Track two rolling values per position — a running max and a running min — since a negative element can flip which one becomes the new best
items.explanation_md: A single running max fails the moment a negative number appears, because it can turn the smallest running product into the largest one — both extremes must be carried forward.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the one-pass max-product-subarray solution that tracks a running max and min?
options: O(n log n) time, O(1) space
items.explanation_md: One pass over the array, two rolling scalars — O(n) time, O(1) space.
